title: Leveraging Correlated Decoding for Bias-Tailored Compass Codes
authors: Arianna Meinking, Julie Campos, Kenneth R. Brown
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is often implemented on hardware that experiences biased noise, where dephasing errors occur more frequently than other errors. This has motivated many recent efforts to develop bias-tailored QEC codes, such as the Clifford-deformed compass codes: a family of codes that achieve high thresholds under biased dephasing noise. We perform circuit-level simulations of the Clifford-deformed elongated compass codes under a biased noise model and evaluate code thresholds using standard minimum weight perfect matching (MWPM) and correlated MWPM. We find that correlated decoding enhances thresholds for all noise biases relative to standard MWPM under circuit-level noise. Our results demonstrate that correlated decoding leads to a higher relative gain in thresholds compared to standard MWPM when applied to codes with asymmetric stabilizers under biased noise.
summary: This paper demonstrates that using correlated decoding techniques can significantly boost the performance of quantum error correction for codes specifically designed for biased noise. By accounting for the relationship between X and Z errors, the researchers found higher error thresholds in realistic circuit-level noise environments.
main_problem: Improving the error-correction thresholds of Clifford-deformed compass codes under biased circuit-level noise by leveraging correlations between X and Z errors.
main_result: Correlated decoding significantly enhances error thresholds for all noise biases under circuit-level noise, with the relative gain being higher for codes with asymmetric stabilizers.
method: Large-scale Monte Carlo simulations using Stim and PyMatching, comparing standard MWPM with correlated MWPM (two-stage and hyperedge-informed) using conditional probabilities.
model_or_system: Clifford-deformed elongated compass codes (specifically the ZXXZ deformation) subject to an asymmetric Pauli noise channel (biased noise) and circuit-level noise models.
key_observables: Error thresholds (p_th), logical error rates for X and Z memories, and the complementary gap (g) for decoder confidence.
important_parameters: Noise bias (eta), elongation parameter (l), code distance (d), and decoding order (ZX vs XZ).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a biased noise model typical of platforms like trapped ions or superconducting qubits and relies on the accuracy of the estimated conditional error probabilities.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the code schematic; Figure 2 depicts the deformed stabilizer and its extraction circuit; Figure 3 shows thresholds for different decoding orders; Figure 4 compares thresholds for different decoders; Figure 5 plots absolute and relative gains vs bias; Figure 6 shows gate ordering to avoid hook errors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of biased noise in QEC, describes the Clifford-deformed compass code structure, details the correlated decoding algorithm and noise models, presents simulation results comparing thresholds and gains, and concludes with performance analysis across different parameters.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of an elongated compass code with elongation ℓ= 3 of distance d = 7. X stabilizers are blue, Z stabilizers are red. The qubits are at the vertices of the lattice. We apply the ZXXZ□Clifford deformation on this code by applying Hadamard transformation on the yellow qubits. The weight-2 X stabilizers detect the location of Z errors with high accuracy, leading to larger thresholds under noise models with dominant Z errors.

Figure 2. 2a) Depiction of a weight-6 Z stabilizer of ZXXZ□-deformed elongated compass code with an elongation of ℓ= 3. Data qubits (qi) and syndrome qubit (s) are labeled. Data qubits are colored yellow if they undergo a Hadamard transformation. 2b) Circuit diagram of syndrome extraction for stabilizer drawn in 2a. We do not include the readout stage in this figure. Readout in our circuit happens for each syndrome qubit at the end of each of d rounds.

Figure 3. CSS correlated decoding in (a), (b) compared to PyMatching correlated decoding in (c) and MWPM in (d). (a) The thresholds of the compass codes with CSS correlated decoding over bias η correcting X errors, then Z errors. (b) The thresholds for CORRZX (Z error then X error) decoding at each ℓ. (c) PyMatching correlated decoder thresholds over a range of η. To achieve a similar to code-capacity decoding scheme with the PyMatching correlated decoder, we use d rounds of memory with the Pauli channel in section II acting on each data qubit before the rounds (d) Standard MWPM thresholds compared to the bias η for different ℓ.

Figure 4. The circuit-level correlated decoding of the Clifford-deformed elongated compass codes and the standard MWPM directly compared over η. (a) The CSS elongated compass codes threshold compared to bias η with MWPM, and (b) with correlated MWPM. (c), (d) The thresholds of the ZXXZ□-deformed code over a range of η with MWPM in (c) and correlated MWPM in (d). The surface code (ℓ= 2 CSS) experiences no change with η.

Figure 5. Threshold gain (∆CORR) and relative gain (∆CORR/pMW P M th ) of correlated decoding compared to standard MWPM over a range of biases η for CSS and ZXXZ□-deformed elongated compass codes. The CSS codes experience tradeoffs in threshold since high stabilizer asymmetry is preferred to maximize correlated decoding gains. The ∆CORR of ZXXZ□codes with increasing η, though the maximum gain is achieved by the CSS codes. The relative correlated decoding gain, ∆CORR/pMW P M th , plateaus at high η for both the CSS and ZXXZ□-deformed codes. The relative gain of the correlated decoder on the ZXXZ□-deformed compass codes improves with elongation for all biases.
